course_info.title: Health Informatics Capstone
course_info.meeting_schedule: Wednesday - 2 hours seminar + independent project work

learning_objectives: Understand capstone expectations, milestones, and deliverables
Select a capstone project topic that integrates multiple health informatics competencies
Begin formulating the project scope and objectives
readings: Sample capstone project proposals from prior years

lecture_notes.introduction: The capstone is your opportunity to demonstrate mastery of health informatics by addressing a real-world problem. You will define a project, execute it over the semester, and present your work professionally.
key_concepts: Capstone project types: system design, implementation evaluation, analytics solution, policy analysis, startup proposal
Industry partner projects: working with a healthcare organization on a real problem
Project scope: achievable within one semester while demonstrating breadth and depth
Milestone schedule: proposal, progress reviews, draft report, final presentation
Faculty advisor and industry mentor roles
lecture_notes.demonstration: Presentation of exemplary capstone projects from prior cohorts, followed by brainstorming session for project ideas

review.value: Projects must integrate at least 3 health informatics competency areas and produce a tangible deliverable beyond a research paper
Students are encouraged to partner with healthcare organizations for real-world projects; faculty can help facilitate connections

learning_objectives: Write a formal capstone project proposal
Define measurable project objectives and success criteria
Create a realistic project timeline with milestones
readings: Selected readings specific to each student's topic area

lecture_notes.introduction: A strong proposal is the foundation for a successful capstone. Today we work through the proposal structure and peer-review draft proposals.
key_concepts: Problem statement: clearly articulating the health informatics challenge
Literature review: situating the project within existing knowledge
Methodology: specific approach, tools, and techniques to be used
Deliverables: tangible outputs (system, analysis, framework, prototype)
Timeline: Gantt chart with weekly milestones aligned to course schedule
lecture_notes.demonstration: Peer review workshop: students present 5-minute elevator pitches of their project ideas and receive structured feedback

review.value: Clear articulation of the specific health informatics problem being addressed, its significance, and why existing solutions are insufficient
Working prototype, analytics dashboard, implementation plan, policy framework, evaluation report with actionable recommendations

class_schedule.topic: Progress Review 1: Background Research and Design
learning_objectives: Present background research and literature review findings
Share initial design decisions and rationale
Receive and incorporate faculty and peer feedback
readings: Project-specific literature as assigned by faculty advisor

lecture_notes.introduction: This milestone ensures you have a solid foundation of background research and a clear design direction before committing to implementation. Peer feedback at this stage is invaluable.
key_concepts: Literature synthesis: organizing findings into a coherent narrative supporting the project
Design decisions: technology choices, methodology selection, stakeholder considerations
Feasibility assessment: confirming the project can be completed within remaining time
Feedback incorporation: distinguishing critical feedback from stylistic preferences
Scope adjustment: narrowing or refining scope based on initial findings
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student presents a 10-minute progress update with 5 minutes of feedback from faculty and peers

review.value: Not just summarizing sources but identifying themes, gaps, and how existing work informs and justifies the capstone project approach
It is better to deliver a focused, high-quality project than an ambitious project that is incomplete at the end of the semester

class_schedule.topic: Progress Review 2: Implementation and Preliminary Results
learning_objectives: Demonstrate substantial progress on project implementation
Present preliminary results or prototype demonstrations
Address challenges encountered and adjustments made

lecture_notes.introduction: By this milestone, you should have substantial implementation progress to share. Preliminary results, even if incomplete, help validate your approach and identify needed adjustments.
key_concepts: Implementation progress: demonstrating working components or completed analysis phases
Preliminary findings: early results that validate or challenge initial hypotheses
Challenge documentation: what went wrong, what was learned, how the approach was adjusted
Remaining work: honest assessment of what is left and whether the timeline is realistic
Quality assurance: testing, validation, and verification of results
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student presents a 15-minute progress update with working demonstration or preliminary data analysis

review.value: Early findings that demonstrate the project approach is working, even if the full analysis is not complete
Faculty value honest reporting of challenges and adjustments over polished presentations that hide problems

learning_objectives: Deliver a professional capstone presentation to faculty and industry guests
Demonstrate the completed capstone project deliverables
Reflect on lessons learned and professional growth

lecture_notes.introduction: The capstone showcase is your opportunity to present your work to a professional audience including faculty, industry partners, and fellow students. Treat this as a professional presentation.
key_concepts: Presentation structure: problem, approach, results, impact, lessons learned
Live demonstration: showing the working deliverable to the audience
Questions and defense: answering probing questions about methodology and decisions
Professional polish: slide design, time management, confident delivery
Portfolio preparation: how to present capstone work to future employers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student delivers a 20-minute formal presentation followed by 10 minutes of Q&A from faculty panel and industry guests

review.value: Ability to answer detailed questions about your methodology, justify your design decisions, and acknowledge limitations demonstrates true mastery
A well-executed capstone project is the centerpiece of your professional portfolio for job applications in health informatics

major_assignments.title: Capstone Project
major_assignments.description: Complete a comprehensive health informatics capstone project integrating multiple competency areas. The project must address a real-world health informatics challenge and produce tangible, professional-quality deliverables.
objectives: Integrate knowledge from across the health informatics curriculum
Address a real-world health informatics challenge
Demonstrate project management and communication skills
deliverables: Project proposal (week 2): 8-10 pages with literature review, methodology, and timeline
Progress report 1 (week 3): background research and design documentation
Progress report 2 (week 4): implementation progress with preliminary results
Final report (week 5): 25-35 pages comprehensive project documentation
Working deliverable: prototype, dashboard, analysis, framework, or plan
Final presentation (week 5): 20-minute professional presentation with live demo

criteria: Addresses a meaningful health informatics problem
Clear value proposition for the target audience
Integrates at least 3 health informatics competency areas
Innovation or novel contribution to the field
Rigorous methodology appropriate to the project type
High-quality implementation or analysis
Appropriate use of health informatics tools and standards
Deliverable is functional and professionally produced
Comprehensive final report with all required sections
Thorough literature review situating the project
Honest discussion of limitations and future work
Professional academic writing with proper citations
Professional and engaging presentation delivery
Effective live demonstration of deliverables
Ability to answer questions and defend decisions
Time management and visual aid quality
Milestone deadlines met consistently
Feedback incorporated constructively
Independent initiative demonstrated
Professional communication with advisor and stakeholders
Meaningful project addressing a real health informatics challenge
High-quality technical execution with professional deliverables
Comprehensive documentation with rigorous analysis
Professional presentation and ability to defend work

project_proposal.description: Quality and feasibility of the capstone project proposal

progress_reviews.description: Demonstrated progress at each milestone with constructive response to feedback

final_deliverable.description: Quality, functionality, and professionalism of the capstone deliverable

final_report.description: Comprehensive written documentation of the project

final_presentation.description: Professional presentation and defense of the capstone project

course_policies.attendance: Weekly seminar sessions are mandatory. Individual meetings with faculty advisor as scheduled. Presentation sessions require attendance for all student presentations.
course_policies.late_submissions: Milestone deadlines are firm. Late milestone submissions lose 5% per day. Final report and presentation dates are non-negotiable.
course_policies.collaboration: Capstone projects are individual. Peer feedback during reviews is expected and valued. Industry partner collaboration is encouraged.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Code, analysis, and writing must be the student's own. External tools and libraries must be properly attributed.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Varies by project. Students must identify and secure technology requirements in the proposal phase. Faculty and IT support can assist with resource access.
